project.title: Purification of Human β and γ Actin from Budding Yeast
project.projectDescription: To facilitate biochemical studies of human cytoplasmic actins, Saccharomyces cerevisiaestrains were developed that express either human β-actin or γ-actin as their sole actin.  Actins purified from these strains have biochemical properties similar to mammalian skeletal muscle α-actin, but display differential binding to some key actin regulators. Mass-spectrometry analysis shows that the human actins made from yeast generally retain their N-terminal methionines, which are modified by acetylation.
project.sampleProcessingProtocol: β and γ actins were purified from yeast via DNAseI affinity chromatography; α-actin was purified from rabbit skeletal muscle acetone powder using conventional methods. Aliquots of 15-30 ug were reduced, alkylated, and digested in-solution with trypsin. The digests were cleaned up by mixed-mode cation exchange stage tips. Approximately 500 ng of each sample was analyzed by shotgun LC-MS/MS. The LC column was 100 um fused silica packed with 1.9 um core shell C18AQ particles to 32 cm, and held at 50C. The MS was an Orbitrap Lumos, operating in data-dependent mode, with MS2 spectra being acquired in the Orbitrap using stepped collision energy and 2 microscans.
project.dataProcessingProtocol: The MS data was searched using SequestHT in Proteome Discoverer (v2.4) simultaneously against three databases: S cerevisiae or rabbit, a list of common laboratory contaminant proteins, and a custom list of human β-actin, γ-actin, and rabbit α actin. Additional entries for all three actins were made with truncations of the first one, two and three N-terminal residues. Enzyme specificity for trypsin was set to semi-tryptic with up to 2 missed cleavages. Precursor and product ion mass tolerances were 10 ppm and 0.02 Da, respectively. Cysteine carbamidomethylation was set as a fixed modification. Methionine oxidation, protein N-terminal acetylation, peptide N-terminal arginylation and in some cases, lysine acetylation or methylation of lysine, histidine and arginine were set as variable modifications. The output was filtered using the Percolator algorithm with strict FDR set to 0.01.
